title: AIACTA ai-attribution.txt Parsed Fields
description:
Describes the structured object produced by parsing an ai-attribution.txt file (Proposal 4, §5.3–5.6). The file format on disk is a plain-text key: value format — this schema describes the parsed JavaScript/Python object returned by the parser, using the canonical hyphenated field names defined in §5.3.

NOTE ON TERMINOLOGY:
  'Publisher' in AIACTA = the content creator who owns the website.
  'AI Provider' in AIACTA = the AI company (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, etc.).
  This file is placed by the PUBLISHER on their own website to declare their preferences to AI Providers.

Schema-Version.description: Version of the ai-attribution.txt specification. Must be '1.0' for this version. Required field — parsers must reject files with an unknown version (§5.6).

Contact.description: One or more contact points for the publisher — email addresses or URLs for licensing/attribution inquiries. Multiple Contact: lines are allowed and accumulate as an array. Example values: 'licensing@example.com', 'https://example.com/ai-licensing'. Strongly recommended.

Preferred-Attribution.description: How the publisher prefers to be credited in AI responses when their content is cited. Example: 'Example Media (example.com)'. AI providers should use this exact string when displaying source attribution.

Canonical-Author.description: The default author attribution for content on this domain when no per-article author is specified. Example: 'The Editorial Team'. Used by AI providers in author-title-url citation format.

items.description: training = model training; rag = real-time retrieval for query answering; index = general indexing; quality-eval = benchmarks/RLHF.

Allow-Purpose.description: Crawl purposes the publisher explicitly permits. Multiple Allow-Purpose: lines accumulate as an array. If this field is absent, all purposes are permitted by default per §5.5. Note: robots.txt Disallow takes precedence over Allow-Purpose (§5.5).

Disallow-Purpose.description: Crawl purposes the publisher explicitly prohibits. The most common use: 'Disallow-Purpose: training' to block model training while allowing real-time query answering ('rag'). Multiple lines accumulate as an array.

Require-Citation.description: If 'true', AI providers must cite this source by name in any response that uses its content. Note: the parser returns a string ('true'/'false') since the file format is plain text; linter rules handle the boolean coercion.

Require-Source-Link.description: If 'true', citations must include a clickable hyperlink to the source URL, not just a text attribution. Requires Require-Citation: true to be meaningful.

Citation-Format.description: Preferred citation display format when Require-Citation is true. Default: 'title-and-url'. 'author-title-url' includes the Canonical-Author or per-article author byline.

Allow-UTM-Append.description: If 'true', AI providers may append UTM parameters to outbound links to this domain for traffic attribution (Proposal 3, §4.3). Default: false. AI providers must never append UTM params to publishers who have not opted in.

Preferred-UTM-Source.description: Custom utm_source value the publisher prefers when Allow-UTM-Append is true. If omitted, the AI provider uses its own platform identifier (e.g. 'anthropic'). Example: 'mypublication'. Must be URL-safe (no spaces, special chars).

Citation-Webhook.description: HTTPS endpoint where the publisher receives real-time citation webhook events (Proposal 2, §3.4). The AI provider POSTs a signed CitationEvent JSON payload to this URL each time content from this domain is cited. The endpoint must be on the same verified domain. Example: 'https://example.com/webhooks/ai-citations'.

Recrawl-After.description: Minimum interval before an AI crawler should recrawl this domain. Format: integer + unit (h=hours, d=days, w=weeks). Example: '24h', '7d', '2w'. Helps publishers control server load. AI providers should respect this as a courtesy.

Licensing-Contact.description: Email address for licensing inquiries — for publishers who want to negotiate direct licensing agreements with AI providers beyond the AAC framework. Example: 'data-licensing@example.com'.

Licensing-URL.description: URL of the publisher's AI licensing terms page. AI providers interested in a direct commercial licence can visit this page. Example: 'https://example.com/ai-licensing-terms'.

Reward-Tier.description: Publisher's participation level in the AAC reward distribution framework (Proposal 5, §7). 'standard' = opt into regular AAC distributions; 'premium' = higher distribution multiplier tier; 'licensing-only' = prefers direct licensing over AAC pool; 'none' = opts out of AAC distributions.

Content-License.description: SPDX licence identifier for the content on this domain. This determines the distribution multiplier in AAC payouts (§7.5). 'All-Rights-Reserved' (highest multiplier), 'CC-BY-ND-4.0', 'CC-BY-SA-4.0', 'CC-BY-4.0', 'CC0-1.0' (zero multiplier). Must be a valid SPDX identifier or 'All-Rights-Reserved'.

$comment: additionalProperties: true implements the forward-compatibility requirement in §5.6 — parsers must silently ignore unknown fields so future spec versions with new fields do not break existing implementations.
